segments.inferno-17-g1: “Ecco” is a deictic exclamation, introducing something being pointed out: “behold” or “here is.” “La fiera con la coda aguzza” is the noun phrase being presented; “aguzza” agrees in feminine singular with “coda,” not with “fiera.”
segments.inferno-17-g2: “Che” introduces a relative clause referring back to “la fiera.” The present-tense verbs “passa” and “rompe” give the beast a general, defining power: it crosses mountains and breaks through walls and arms; “l’armi” is an archaic/poetic contraction for “le armi.”
segments.inferno-17-g3: Again “Ecco” points emphatically to the creature. “Colei che” means “she who” or “the one who,” and “tutto ’l mondo” is poetic for “tutto il mondo”; “appuzza” is present tense, meaning that she fouls or stinks up the whole world.
segments.inferno-17-g4: This target contains only punctuation and no grammatical words to analyze.
segments.inferno-17-g5: “Sì” here means “so” or “thus,” linking this speech to the preceding exclamation. “Lo mio duca” is archaic/poetic for modern “il mio duca,” and “cominciò a parlarmi” uses “cominciare a” plus infinitive; “parlarmi” is “parlare” with enclitic “mi,” meaning “to speak to me.”
segments.inferno-17-g6: “Accennolle” is a fused poetic form: “accennò” plus “le,” meaning “he signaled to her/it.” “Che venisse” is a subordinate clause with the imperfect subjunctive “venisse,” expressing what Virgil signaled for the beast to do: come to the bank.
segments.inferno-17-g7: This phrase gives the location where the creature is to come. “Vicino al” means “near to the,” “fin” is a shortened poetic form of “fine,” meaning “end,” and “d’i” stands for “di i,” equivalent to modern “dei”; “passeggiati marmi” means the stone or marble surfaces already walked over.
segments.inferno-17-g23: “Nel” contracts “in” + “il,” and “vano” is used substantively here to mean the empty space or void, though that sense is not in the supplied list. The imperfect “guizzava” describes the tail’s continuous writhing or flicking, with “tutta sua coda” meaning “its whole tail” in poetic word order.
segments.inferno-17-g24: “Torcendo” is a gerund linked to “guizzava,” explaining how the tail was moving: by twisting the fork upward. “Ch’” is a relative pronoun referring to “forca,” and “a guisa di scorpion” means “in the manner of a scorpion”; “armava” means that the fork furnished or armed the tail’s point like a sting.
segments.inferno-17-g25: “Lo duca” uses the older masculine article “lo” where modern Italian would normally have “il.” “Disse” is the passato remoto of “dire,” the standard narrative past tense for a completed act of speaking.
segments.inferno-17-g26: “Convien che” is an impersonal expression meaning “it is necessary that,” followed by the subjunctive “torca.” The “si” with “la nostra via” makes the road/path the thing that turns itself or turns aside; “insino a” means “as far as,” and the final “che colà si corca” is a relative clause describing the beast lying over there.
segments.inferno-17-g27: “Però” here has its older causal sense, “therefore,” linking the action to the guide’s instruction. “Scendemmo” is passato remoto, “we descended/went down,” and “a la” is the uncontracted form of modern “alla”; “destra mammella” means the right breast or side of the beast.
segments.inferno-17-g28: “Diece” is an archaic spelling of “dieci,” ten, and “femmo” is from “fare,” meaning “we made/took,” though the supplied lemma and senses do not match this form. “In su lo stremo” means “on/along the extreme edge,” with “lo” as the article before “stremo.”
segments.inferno-17-g29: “Per” introduces purpose: they take those steps “in order to” avoid the dangerous ground. “Cessar” is used transitively in an older sense meaning “avoid” or “keep clear of,” not the modern intransitive “cease”; its objects are “la rena” and “la fiammella.”
segments.inferno-17-g30: “Quando” introduces a temporal clause, and “noi” is explicitly stated for emphasis. “Venuti semo” is an archaic form of “siamo venuti,” literally “we are come,” with “a lei” referring back to the feminine “bestia.”
segments.inferno-17-g31: “Poco più oltre” means “a little farther on,” setting the place where Dante sees the next group. “Veggio” is an archaic first-person present, used vividly in narration, and it governs the infinitive “seder”: “I see people sitting”; “propinqua” agrees with the collective singular “gente.”
segments.inferno-17-g8: "E" links this action to the preceding summons: after being signaled, the creature responds. "Quella sozza imagine di froda" is a noun phrase meaning the monster as the visible embodiment of fraud; "sen venne" is poetic for "se ne venne," a pronominal form meaning "came along/came toward them."
segments.inferno-17-g9: The verb "arrivò" is used transitively/poetically: the creature brought or made reach "la testa e ’l busto" to the edge. "’l" is the contracted article "il," so the coordinated objects are "the head and the torso."
segments.inferno-17-g10: "Ma" contrasts the partial landing just described: the front part comes to the bank, but not the tail. "’n su la" is an archaic contraction of "in su la," meaning "up onto/upon," and "trasse" is the preterite of "trarre," here "pulled" or "drew."
segments.inferno-17-g11: "La faccia sua" uses possessive placement after the noun, common in poetic Italian: "its face." The second "faccia" is again a noun, despite the supplied lemma, and "d’uom giusto" means "of a just/righteous man," describing the deceptive human appearance.
segments.inferno-17-g12: "Tanto" introduces the degree of the creature’s deceptive gentleness: its outer skin looked so benign. "Avea" is the archaic/poetic imperfect form of "aveva," and "di fuor" means "on the outside" or "externally."
segments.inferno-17-g13: This segment completes the contrast with the human-looking face: "e" adds that the whole remaining body was serpentine. The verb "era" is understood from the previous clause: "and all the other trunk [was] of a serpent."
segments.inferno-17-g14: "Avea" again means "had," with the object placed before the adjective: the creature had two "branche," claw-like paws or talons. "Insin l’ascelle" means "as far as/up to the armpits," with "l’" standing for the plural article before "ascelle" in the poetic phrasing.
segments.inferno-17-g15: The objects "lo dosso e ’l petto e ambedue le coste" are fronted before the verb phrase for poetic emphasis. "Dipinti avea" is an inverted construction meaning "it had painted," with "dipinti" agreeing broadly with the preceding body parts; "di nodi e di rotelle" describes the pattern as knots and little round disks.
segments.inferno-17-g16: "Con più color" means "with more colors"; "color" is an apocopated poetic form of "colori," not the demonstrative "those." The phrase introduces a comparison with the creature’s patterned hide from the previous sentence.
segments.inferno-17-g17: "Sommesse e sovraposte" describes colors or patterns laid underneath and overlaid on top. "Non fer mai" is poetic for "non fecero mai," "never made," and the subject comes after the verb: "Tartari né Turchi"; "drappi" here is a noun, "cloths/fabrics."
segments.inferno-17-g18: "Né" continues the negative comparison: not even Arachne produced such woven work. "Fuor" is poetic for "furono," "were," while "imposte" is a past participle meaning "set up" or "laid on" the loom, not the noun meanings listed.
segments.inferno-17-g19: "Come" introduces the first simile, answered later by "così." "Stanno a riva" means "are positioned/lie at the shore," and the subject "i burchi" follows the verb, a normal poetic inversion.
segments.inferno-17-g20: "Che" is relative, referring back to "i burchi." The repeated "parte ... parte" means "partly ... partly," explaining that the barges lie half in water and half on land.
segments.inferno-17-g21: A second simile is added with "e come," again preparing for the later "così." "Lo bivero" is the beaver, and "s’assetta" is reflexive, "settles/positions itself"; "a far sua guerra" means "to wage its own war," a figurative way of describing the animal’s hunting or struggle.
segments.inferno-17-g22: "Così" completes the two comparisons: just so, the beast was positioned partly on the edge. "Si stava" is a reflexive/intensive form of "stare," meaning "was staying/was stationed," and the relative clause "ch’è di pietra e ’l sabbion serra" describes the stone rim that confines the sandy plain.
segments.inferno-17-g62: Or = now. Te ne va is an older/clitic ordering of an imperative telling someone to go away: te (you) + ne (away/from here) + va (imperative form of andare). In modern Italian you would say "vattene" or "va' via"; Dante keeps separate clitics before the verb.
segments.inferno-17-g63: E perché = "and because/since" — perché here introduces a causal clause, not a question. Se’ is the archaic contracted second-person present indicative (sei = you are). Vivo is a predicate adjective meaning "alive." Anco is an archaic adverb meaning "still/yet."
segments.inferno-17-g64: Sappi is the second-person singular imperative of sapere ("know" or "be aware"). Che introduces the subordinate content clause. 'Il mio vicin Vitalïano' is the noun phrase (the definite article 'il' is elided as ’l). Sederà is future tense, 3rd person singular, meaning "will sit/be seated/will take a seat." Qui = here; dal = da + il meaning "at/by the" (here: "at my left side"); mio sinistro fianco = "my left flank/side."
segments.inferno-17-g65: Con = with/among. Questi Fiorentin = "these Florentines" (Fiorentin is plural). Son is the poetic/contracted form of sono (first person singular present: "I am"). Padoano = Paduan (native of Padua) used as predicate noun/adjective.
segments.inferno-17-g66: Spesse fïate = "many times/often" (fïate = occasions/times; the diaeresis marks vowel separation). Mi ’ntronan li orecchi is literal: "the ears deafen me" (li orecchi is subject, ntronan = deafen/stun, mi = to me). Gridando is a present participle "shouting/crying out," modifying those who make the cry.
segments.inferno-17-g67: Vegna is an optative/jussive form (present subjunctive used like "may he come" or "let him come"). L' cavalier sovrano = "the sovereign knight" (article + noun + adjective). The phrase is an exclamation calling for that knight's arrival.
segments.inferno-17-g68: Che introduces a relative clause ("who"). Recherà is future, 3rd singular, "will bring/bear." La tasca = the purse (object). Con tre becchi = "with three beaks" (con = with). The clause describes what the knight will bring.
segments.inferno-17-g69: This segment is punctuation only (a full stop).
segments.inferno-17-g70: Qui = here. Distorse is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of distorgere, meaning "twisted/contorted." La bocca is the direct object. E = and. Di fuor = "out from" or "outward" (di + fuor), and trasse is passato remoto (3rd singular) of trarre/trarre meaning "drew/pulled out." La lingua = the tongue (direct object of trasse).
segments.inferno-17-g71: Come = like/as. Bue = an ox. Che introduces a relative clause meaning "that/which." ’L = il (the). Naso = nose (subject of the subordinate clause). Lecchi is an archaic/poetic verb form (here used as a finite verb meaning "licks"); its exact inflection is older Italian, roughly corresponding to modern third-person singular "lecca."
segments.inferno-17-g32: Quivi is an adverb meaning 'there' and places the utterance; ’l maestro is the subject 'the master' with the elided article 'l'. Acciò che introduces a purpose clause ('so that/so as to') and is followed by a compressed adjectival phrase, tutta piena esperïenza 'a wholly full experience', where tutta (whole) modifies piena (full) + esperïenza (experience). D’ is the contraction of di ('of') and esto giron is an archaic phrase 'this giron/circle' (giron = circle, here the circle of Hell); porti is a present subjunctive/optative form (used to express purpose/wish: 'may you carry' or 'you may bring'), not the noun porto, so the lemma/sense list for porti does not match the verbal use.
segments.inferno-17-g33: mi is the clitic indirect object 'to me', and disse is the simple past 'said' (he said to me). Together they mark the reporting clause introducing the quoted speech.
segments.inferno-17-g34: va is an imperative of andare ('go') addressed to the hearer (tu), here as a direct command inside the quotation.
segments.inferno-17-g35: e is the coordinating 'and'; vedi is another imperative (see/look) in the second person singular commanding the addressee; la + lor is the older ordering of article then possessive ('the their') meaning 'their'; mena here is a noun meaning 'company, train, troop' (their group) — that lexical sense is not in the provided list, so it is marked unavailable.
segments.inferno-17-g36: Li tuoi ragionamenti is a noun phrase 'your remarks/arguments' (li = the). sian is the present subjunctive of essere (sian = 'may be' / 'be' used jussively: 'let them be'); là corti places them 'there short/brief' with corti agreeing in gender/number with ragionamenti.
segments.inferno-17-g37: mentre che is a temporal conjunction 'while' (mentre) with che; torni is second-person singular present (here subjunctive/temporally used) of tornare — 'while you return' or 'until you come back'.
segments.inferno-17-g38: parlerò is the first-person singular future 'I will speak'; con questa means 'with this one' (questa = this woman/thing), indicating whom the speaker will address while the addressee is away.
segments.inferno-17-g39: che introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/desired result; ne is a pronoun 'of it/of them' referring back to the matter under discussion; conceda is present subjunctive (third singular) of concedere expressing a wish/hope 'that she grant'; i suoi omeri forti is 'her/their strong shoulders' with the possessive suo placed after the article in older usage and omeri an archaic plural for shoulders.
segments.inferno-17-g40: Così ancor 'thus again' is a manner adverbial; su per la strema testa literally 'up along the extreme head/crest' (strema = extreme, testa = head/ridge), di quel settimo cerchio = 'of that seventh circle' (identifying the location). tutto solo is an emphatic 'completely alone' and andai is the first-person singular past definite (I went), narrating Dante's movement; word order is poetic but follows logically: manner/place/qualification + verb.
segments.inferno-17-g41: dove is the relative adverb 'where'; sedea is the imperfect 'sat/was seated' (3rd singular), setting a continuous past scene; la gente mesta 'the mournful people' is the subject of that clause, with mesta agreeing in gender/number.
segments.inferno-17-g42: Per + noun here marks the source or channel ('through/from'). Fora is an archaic/poetic word meaning 'out' (placed before the verb), and scoppiava is the imperfect indicative 'was bursting/burst out.' The normal subject lor duolo ('their grief') follows the verb by inverted word order typical of poetry; lor is the enclitic possessive 'their'.
segments.inferno-17-g43: Di qua is an adverbial locution meaning 'on this side / from here'; it is elliptical and standalone to mark direction or locality (paired with di là below).
segments.inferno-17-g44: Di là ('from there') parallels di qua. Soccorrien is a 3rd-person plural present form meaning 'they come to help' or 'they reach to help'; con le mani is an instrument phrase 'with their hands.' Quando introduces the temporal clause 'when toward the vapours' (a' = a + i).
segments.inferno-17-g45: E quando introduces a second temporal alternative ('and when'); al is the contraction a + il ('to/the' or 'at the'), caldo is an adjective preceding the noun suolo ('hot ground'). The colon closes the pair of alternatives.
segments.inferno-17-g46: Non altrimenti ('no differently') negates the comparison that follows. Fan is the literary contracted form of fanno ('they do'); di state is an archaic expression meaning 'in summer' (estate → state). Or (here archaic for ora) means 'now,' repeated to mark alternating actions; col = con + il ('with the') and introduces the instruments ('with the muzzle / with the foot').
segments.inferno-17-g47: Quando introduces the circumstance 'when they are bitten'; son is the literary contraction of sono ('they are') and morsi is a past participle used predicatively ('bitten'). The agent is expressed with da + plural nouns (da pulci, da mosche, da tafani = 'by fleas / flies / horseflies').
segments.inferno-17-g116: A single comma linking this clause to the preceding phrase; no lexical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-17-g117: "che" is a relative pronoun here meaning "which" introducing a clause that characterizes the fear; "fu" is the perfect (historic) of essere, "was"; "la mia" is a feminine singular pronominal phrase "my (one)" (la agrees with the understood feminine noun paura from the context).
segments.inferno-17-g118: "quando" = "when" introduces a temporal clause; "vidi" is the passato remoto (I saw); "ch’ i’ era" = "che io era" (that I was) with the clitic contraction ch'; "ne l’aere d’ogne parte" is a locative phrase meaning "in the air on every side" ("ne" = in; "l’aere" = the air/sky; "ogne parte" = every side/place). Note: "i’" here is the old form for "io" (I).
segments.inferno-17-g119: "e vidi" continues with another past action "and I saw"; "spenta" is a past participle used adjectivally ("extinguished/put out"); "ogne veduta" = "every sight/view"; "fuor che" = "except that" ("fuor" here conveys exception); "de la fera" = "of the beast" ("de" = of). The phrase means he saw all sight extinguished except the beast's.
segments.inferno-17-g120: "Ella sen va" is the pronominal/separable verb "se n' va" (se ne va) = "she goes away/gone off"; "notando" is a gerund modifying the motion (literally "going, noting/marking/moving"), and the duplicated adjective "lenta lenta" is an adverbial repetition for emphasis meaning "slowly, slowly." Together they describe manner of motion.
segments.inferno-17-g121: "rota" is used here as a verbal/adjectival root meaning "turning/whirling" (poetic use of the noun 'rota' as a verbal image) and "discende" is the 3rd person singular present of discendere "descends." The two verbs describe successive motions: she turns and descends.
segments.inferno-17-g122: Main clause: "ma non me n'accorgo" = "but I do not notice it," where "me" is the first-person object, "n'" = ne ("of it/about it"), and "accorgo" is present "I notice/realize." "Se non che" is an idiomatic concessive/exclusive connector meaning "except that"; "al viso e di sotto mi venta" literally "it blows to my face and beneath me," with "mi" as indirect object and "venta" (ventare) meaning "blows/fans."
segments.inferno-17-g123: "Io sentia" uses the imperfect sentia (I felt/heard) describing a past, ongoing perception; "già" = already. "da la man destra" = "from the right hand/side" (prepositional source). "il gorgo far ... un orribile scroscio" is an object-clause with the infinitive "far" (to make) governed by the perceptive verb: the whirlpool (il gorgo) makes (far) beneath us (far sotto noi) a horrible crash (un orribile scroscio). Note the infinitive clause expresses what was perceived.
segments.inferno-17-g124: "per che" = "wherefore/so/for which reason" introduces the result; "con li occhi 'n giù" = "with (my) eyes downward" (a prepositional phrase indicating gaze-direction), and "la testa sporgo" is a 1st-person present "sporgo" (I thrust/lean out/forward) with "la testa" as the direct object: "I thrust my head forward."
segments.inferno-17-g125: "Allor" = then; "fu' io" is the literary/historical perfect of essere ("I was") with inverted order (fu' io rather than io fui). "più timido" = "more timid" (comparative/adverbial sense), and "a lo stoscio" is a locative phrase "at the edge/brink" (a + lo = at the). The clause describes a change of disposition at a place.
segments.inferno-17-g126: "però ch' i' vidi fuochi e senti' pianti" uses "però ch'" as a causal conjunction "because/for that"; "i' vidi" = "I saw" (historic past), "fuochi" = fires; "e senti'" = "and I heard" (past form of sentire), and "pianti" = lamentations/cries. The clause gives the reason for his fear. Note the archaic contractions (ch', i').
segments.inferno-17-g127: "ond'" (onde/ond') = "therefore/whence/so" (a connective adverb); "io tremando" uses the gerund tremando ("trembling") co‑ordinated with the subject "io"; "tutto" = entirely/completely; "mi raccoscio" is reflexive present "I huddle up/crouch up" (mi = myself). The clause states the consequent physical reaction, with the gerund giving manner.
segments.inferno-17-g128: E (and) + vidi (passato remoto of vedere) + poi (then): a simple main clause "And I then saw..." with the subject io omitted (typical in Italian). 'Vidi' is a completed past tense (passato remoto) used narratively.
segments.inferno-17-g129: ché introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for/because'); nol is the contracted non + lo = 'not it' (object clitic attached to non); vedea is the imperfect indicative ('I/he was seeing' / 'did see') indicating an ongoing or background perception ('I had not been seeing it'); davanti means 'ahead' or 'in front'.
segments.inferno-17-g130: lo scendere e ’l girar: lo and the elided l' before the infinitives nominalize them ('the descending and the circling'); scendere and girar are infinitives used as nouns. per li gran mali = 'through/among the great evils' (per + definite article li); che introduces the relative clause; s’appressavan is the imperfect ('si' reflexive enclitic + appressavan) meaning 'were approaching'; da diversi canti = 'from different quarters/sides' (canti = corners/quarters).
segments.inferno-17-g131: Come introduces a simile ('as/like'). '’l falcon' = the falcon; ch’è stato assai su l’ali is a relative clause: ch’ = che 'that', è stato is the compound (perfect) 'has been', assai = 'much/for a long time', su l'ali = 'on the wings' — the clause describes the falcon's prolonged position.
segments.inferno-17-g132: che introduces a further clause describing the falcon's behavior: sanza veder = 'without seeing' (sanza = without + infinitive veder); logoro 'lure/decoy' or uccello 'prey/quarry' are the objects omitted by sanza; fa dire is the causative construction (fa + infinitive) 'causes (someone) to say' and al falconiere marks the person to whom the utterance is attributed; Omè is an exclamation ('alas').
segments.inferno-17-g133: tu cali is the falconer's direct address: tu (you) + cali (present indicative, 2nd singular of calare). The present is used here as an exclamatory/imperative-like comment ('you descend/fall!').
segments.inferno-17-g134: Punctuation only (comma) continuing the simile and narrative; no words to analyze.
segments.inferno-17-g135: discende (present indicative 'descends' or 'comes down') + lasso (an adverb/descriptor meaning 'down/low' or 'weary' in older Italian) + onde introduces a clause indicating origin/way ('whence' or 'from which') and si move isnello = 'it moves itself nimble' with si reflexive + move (present) and isnello 'nimble/quick'. Together: 'it descends down and from there moves lightly.'
segments.inferno-17-g136: per cento rote = 'by/through a hundred wheels' where per = 'by/through' and rote is plural of 'rota' ('wheels' or heavenly spheres); this gives a sense of many circular motions or revolutions.
segments.inferno-17-g137: e da lunge si pone dal suo maestro: e (and); da lunge = 'from far away' (distance); si pone = 'it sets/places itself' with reflexive si (here 'puts itself'); dal suo maestro = 'away from its master'. The verb pone (porre) is present indicative describing the falcon/thing drawing away.
segments.inferno-17-g138: disdegnoso e fello are two adjectives ('disdainful and fell/vengeful') qualifying the subject; fello is an archaic adjective meaning cruel or treacherous.
segments.inferno-17-g139: così ne puose al fondo Gerïone: così = 'thus'; ne is the pronominal particle referring back to 'us/there' (object/partitive 'of it/them'); puose is passato remoto of porre ('placed/put'); al fondo = 'to the bottom'; Gerïone is the subject (Geryon). The following al piè al piè de la stagliata rocca is a locative repetition ('at the very foot, at the foot of the sheer rock') emphasizing position.
segments.inferno-17-g140: e = 'and', a simple coordinating conjunction continuing the sentence.
segments.inferno-17-g141: discarcate le nostre persone: discarcate is a past participle (unloaded/disembarked) used with the following noun phrase; the phrase functions as a participial clause ('having unloaded our persons/our people'), with le nostre persone 'our people/our persons' as its direct object.
segments.inferno-17-g142: si dileguò is reflexive + passato remoto (dileguò) = 'he/it vanished' or 'dissolved itself'; come da corda cocca is a simile 'like/from a cord's notch' (come = like/as, da = from, corda = rope, cocca an old term for notch/nock). The whole clause means 'he vanished like from a rope's notch.'
segments.inferno-17-g48: "Poi che" is a temporal conjunction meaning "after/when." The clause has inverted, somewhat poetic word order: "nel viso a certi li occhi porsi" literally places the location and indirect object before the verb porsi (to place/turn); here porsi is used reflexively/passively to mean "the eyes were turned" toward the face. "li" is the archaic plural article "the."
segments.inferno-17-g49: "ne' quali" = "in which" introduces a relative phrase describing those eyes; "'l doloroso foco" is a poetic noun phrase "the sorrowful/fire of suffering." The verb "casca" (from cascāre) here means "falls" — an archaic/poetic present form used of the flame dropping into the eyes. (The provided sense list for "casca" does not match this meaning.)
segments.inferno-17-g50: Negative statement: "non ne conobbi alcun" = "I knew/recognized none of them." "ne" is the partitive pronoun "of them/among them," and "conobbi" is the past tense (passato remoto) "I recognized/knew." "alcun" here functions in the negative sense "any/none."
segments.inferno-17-g51: "Ma io m'accorsi che..." = "But I noticed that..." "m'accorsi" is the passato remoto of the pronominal verb "accorgersi" (I perceived/noticed). The subordinate clause "dal collo a ciascun pendea una tasca" uses inverted order: literally "from the neck to each hung a pouch," i.e. "a pouch hung from each one's neck." The relative "ch'avea certo colore e certo segno" modifies "tasca."
segments.inferno-17-g52: "e quindi par che" = "and therefore it seems that..." introduces an apparentive clause. "loro occhio" uses the singular "occhio" to mean each one's gaze; "si pasca" (pascere used reflexively) means "feeds/feasts on itself" or more naturally "takes pleasure in" — here "their eye feasts on it." The verb form after "par che" is presented as a dependent clause expressing appearance.
segments.inferno-17-g53: "E com' io riguardando tra lor vegno" is a circumstantial clause: "As I, looking among them, came (on)." "riguardando" is a gerund/participle "looking," and "vegno" (from venire) is a present/historic narrative form "I come/arrive" used here to narrate the action of coming upon something while looking.
segments.inferno-17-g54: "in una borsa gialla vidi azzurro" = "in a yellow bag I saw an azzurro (one)." "vidi" is passato remoto "I saw." Here "azzurro" is used as a noun (the blue/azure one). The relative clause "che d'un leone avea faccia e contegno" modifies that azure one: "which had the face and comportment (bearing) of a lion." "avea" is the imperfect of avere.
segments.inferno-17-g55: "Poi" is the simple sequencer "then/afterward," used here to move the narrative forward.
segments.inferno-17-g56: "procedendo di mio sguardo il curro" is a metaphorical phrase: "the chariot/track (curro) of my glance proceeding" — i.e. "as my gaze continued its course." Dante uses an image (curro) for the motion of the eye; word order places the participle before the image for poetic effect.
segments.inferno-17-g57: "vidine un'altra" is an older/poetic form of "vidi ne/vidi un'altra" meaning "I saw another of them." "come sangue rossa" is a simile: "as red as blood," with the adjective after the noun for emphasis.
segments.inferno-17-g58: "mostrando" (present participle) links the action: "showing/revealing." "un'oca bianca più che burro" uses a comparative idiom "whiter than butter" (più che = "more than/than"), so the phrase means "displaying a goose whiter than butter." The participle relates back to the creature seen.
segments.inferno-17-g59: "E un che d'una scrofa azzurra e grossa segnato avea lo suo sacchetto bianco" is a relative clause: "and one who had marked his white little pouch with a blue, fat sow." Note the past construction "segnato avea" (had marked) where the participle precedes the auxiliary — a common poetic order in Dante — and the relative "che" = "who/that."
segments.inferno-17-g60: "mi disse" is the simple past reporting clause "he said to me." "mi" is the indirect object pronoun "to me," and "disse" is passato remoto "said."
segments.inferno-17-g61: Direct question: "Che fai tu in questa fossa?" = "What are you doing in this pit?" Word order is emphatic ("Che fai tu" rather than "Che fai?"), with "che" functioning as interrogative "what."
segments.inferno-17-g88: ‘I’ is the subject clitic for 'io' (I). 'm’ asssettai' = 'mi assettai' is passato remoto (first person singular) of a reflexive verb 'assettare' here meaning 'seat oneself' — literally 'I seated myself'. 'in su' is a two-part prepositional phrase meaning 'upon'/'on top of', and 'quelle spallacce' is a demonstrative + augmentative noun phrase: 'those (quello) big shoulders'.
segments.inferno-17-g89: 'sì' here means 'so'/'thus'. 'volli' is passato remoto of volere (I wanted); it indicates the speaker's intention in the past. 'dir' is the infinitive 'to say' and completes 'volli' (I wished to say).
segments.inferno-17-g90: 'ma' is the adversative conjunction 'but'. 'la voce' = 'the voice' (direct object). 'non venne' is passato remoto of venire negated: 'did not come' (the sound/voice did not issue). 'com’ io credetti' = 'as I believed/expected': 'com’' = 'come' ('as'), 'io' subject pronoun, 'credetti' = passato remoto of credere ('I thought/believed').
segments.inferno-17-g91: Quoted imperative: 'Fa' is the imperative (or second-person singular present of fare used as a command) = 'make/do'. 'che' is a subordinator introducing the subordinate clause. 'tu' is the explicit subject 'you'. 'm’' is the clitic object 'mi' meaning 'me' (enclitic before the verb), and 'abbracce' is the present subjunctive/imperative form of 'abbracciare' (to embrace) addressed to 'tu' — 'that you embrace me.'
segments.inferno-17-g92: 'Ma' = 'but' introducing contrast. 'esso' is a demonstrative/pronoun referring back to the creature ('he/it'); here it functions as the subject 'he'.
segments.inferno-17-g93: This relative clause modifies 'esso'. 'ch’' = 'che' functioning as 'who/which'. 'altra volta' = 'another time'. 'mi sovvenne' uses 'sovvenire' in the impersonal/personal sense 'came to me'/'occurred to me' (passato remoto). 'ad altro forse' = 'perhaps for something else' ('ad' + 'altro', 'for something else', with 'forse' = 'perhaps').
segments.inferno-17-g94: 'tosto' = 'soon'/'as soon as'. 'ch’ i’ montai' = 'that I mounted' (passato remoto 'montai'); the clause means 'as soon as I had climbed/was up'. 'con le braccia m’avvinse' = 'he clasped me with his arms' — 'm’' is the clitic object 'mi' (to me), 'avvinse' past of 'avvingere/avvinse' = 'clasp/embrace'. 'e mi sostenne' = 'and supported me' with 'sostenne' (past of sostenere).
segments.inferno-17-g95: 'e disse' = 'and he said' (disse = passato remoto of dire). The verb introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-17-g96: Proper name vocative 'Gerïon' (Geryon) — the speaker is addressing the beast by name.
segments.inferno-17-g97: 'moviti' is an imperative or present-instructive form with clitic 'ti' (move yourself / stir), directed to Gerïon. 'omai' = 'now'/'at once' (an adverb strengthening the command).
segments.inferno-17-g98: 'le rote larghe' = 'the broad wheels'; 'le' is the feminine plural article, 'rote' = wheels (or rounds), 'larghe' = adjective 'wide/broad' modifying 'rote'.
segments.inferno-17-g99: 'e' = 'and'. 'lo scender' = the descent (infinitive used as noun: 'the going down'); 'sia' is present subjunctive of essere used optatively/commanding here ('let it be'); 'poco' = 'small'/'little' — 'let the descent be slight.'
segments.inferno-17-g100: 'pensa' is an imperative (think/consider) addressed to Gerïon. 'la nova soma' = 'the new load/burden' ('nova' = new, 'soma' = load). 'che tu hai' is a relative clause 'that you have' (present of avere). The whole sentence: 'think of the new burden that you have.'
segments.inferno-17-g101: 'Come' = 'as' introducing a simile. 'la navicella' = 'the little ship' (diminutive). 'esce' is present of uscire 'goes out/leaves'. 'di loco' = 'from its place' (lit. 'from the place/berth'). The doubled phrase 'in dietro in dietro' emphasizes repeated backward motion: 'backwards and backwards' (i.e., by backing up).
segments.inferno-17-g102: 'sì' = 'so/thus'. 'quindi' = 'from there'/'then' indicating movement away from the place mentioned. 'si tolse' is a reflexive use of togliere in the passato remoto: literally 'took itself away' = 'withdrew'/'moved off'.
segments.inferno-17-g103: 'e poi' = 'and then'. 'ch’ al tutto si sentì a gioco' = 'when/after it felt entirely at ease' — 'al tutto' = 'completely', 'si sentì' = passato remoto of sentirsi 'felt itself', and 'a gioco' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'at play/ease' (i.e., free to move).
segments.inferno-17-g104: 'là' = 'there'. '’v’ era' = contracted 'vi era' = 'was there' (imperfect of essere). '’l petto' = 'the chest' (petto) — location adverbial: 'where the breast/chest was'.
segments.inferno-17-g105: 'la coda' = 'the tail' (subject). 'rivolse' is passato remoto of rivolgere, here meaning 'turned toward' — i.e., it turned its tail toward that place.
segments.inferno-17-g106: 'e' = 'and'. 'quella tesa' = 'that stretched (part)' — 'tesa' (from 'teso') used as adjective meaning 'stretched out/taught', referring to the extended tail or posterior part.
segments.inferno-17-g107: 'come' = 'like/as'. 'anguilla' = 'an eel' — a simile comparing the movement of the stretched part to an eel's slithering motion.
segments.inferno-17-g108: 'mosse' is passato remoto of muovere = 'it moved' or 'it stirred' (the stretched part moved).
segments.inferno-17-g109: 'e con le branche' = 'and with its branches/claws/limbs' — 'branche' here denotes the grasping limbs or claws of the beast. 'l’aere a sé raccolse' = 'it gathered the air to itself' — 'aere' = 'air', 'a sé' = 'to itself', 'raccolse' = passato remoto of raccogliere/ raccogliersi = 'gathered/drew in.'
segments.inferno-17-g110: This is the main comparative clause. 'Maggior paura' (greater fear) is fronted for emphasis. 'non credo che fosse' uses 'credo' in the present (I think) with the imperfect subjunctive 'fosse' (that there was), a customary poetic sequence after 'credo che' to express the speaker's judgement. The temporal clause 'quando Fetonte abbandonò li freni' (when Phaethon abandoned/let go the reins) uses the passato remoto 'abbandonò' for a single past action; 'li' is an archaic/stylistic masculine plural article for 'the reins'.
segments.inferno-17-g111: 'per che' here acts as a connective meaning 'whereby/therefore' (literally 'by which/through which') introducing the result of Feton's action. The article is elided as '’l' (poetic contraction of 'il') and 'ciel' is the archaic form of 'cielo' (sky/heaven).
segments.inferno-17-g112: 'come pare ancor' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'as it still appears/seems'. 'come' = 'as', 'pare' = present 'seems', and 'ancor' is the archaic/poetic form of 'ancora' (still/yet). It qualifies the speaker's perception of the following verbal phrase.
segments.inferno-17-g113: The reflexive clitic 'si' plus the past-tense verb 'cosse' yields a middle/passive sense: roughly 'was seamed/stitched' or 'was sewn up' (a poetic expression describing the sky's condition). 'Si' marks that the action affects the subject; 'cosse' is a terse, archaic/poetic past form in third person singular that completes the result introduced by 'per che'.
segments.inferno-17-g114: This clause begins with 'né quando' = 'nor when', introducing the second fearful example. 'Icaro misero' places the adjective 'misero' (wretched) after the proper name for a traditional, emphatic effect. The object 'le reni' (the loins/flanks) is placed before the verb 'sentì' (he felt), and 'sentì' + infinitive 'spennar' expresses a sensory perception ('felt [them] being plucked'). 'per la scaldata cera' gives the cause/agent phrase ('by/through the heated/melted wax').
segments.inferno-17-g115: 'gridando' is a gerund meaning 'shouting' and links to the main clause as a concurrent action. 'il padre a lui' = 'the father to him' (the father addressing his son). The quoted exclamation 'Mala via tieni!' uses 'tieni' (2nd-person singular present) in an idiomatic sense: 'you hold/take a bad road' = 'you're taking the wrong path.' The terse word order and present verb give immediacy to the father's cry.
segments.inferno-17-g72: 'E io,' is a simple coordinating phrase: E = 'and', with io ('I') used here as an emphatic subject pronoun set off by a comma to mark the speaker preparing to describe his action.
segments.inferno-17-g73: Temendo is a gerund ('fearing') that introduces a subordinate clause of fear; the archaic particle no (= non) combines with the following infinitive phrase to yield a negative fearing construction (literally 'fearing that not...'), so crucciasse is an imperfect-subjunctive used in such clauses to express a feared possibility ('might be upset'). '’l più star' is a compact expression where l' (= lo/the neuter idea of 'staying') + più = 'staying any longer'; 'lui che di poco star m’avea ’mmonito' is a relative clause 'he who had a short while ago admonished me' (di poco = 'a little while ago', m’avea = mi aveva 'had warned me'). Together: 'fearing that staying any longer might upset him — he who had warned me a little while before.'
segments.inferno-17-g74: torna’mi is an older/dialectal fusional form meaning 'I turned (myself) back' (torna' = tornai/tornei-like past form with the enclitic mi = 'to me/myself'); in dietro = 'backwards' or 'back'; da + l'anime lasse = 'to/among the weary souls' (da here indicates motion toward someone in compounds like 'tornare da qualcuno'). The whole phrase: 'I turned back to the weary souls.'
segments.inferno-17-g75: Trova’ is an archaic past form (trovò / trovava) 'found' or 'met'; 'il duca mio' = 'my duke' (here 'duca' = Virgil, i.e. my guide). The clause ch’era salito già su la groppa del fiero animale is a relative clause: era salito is a perfect/past (auxiliary essere + past participle) meaning 'had climbed,' già = 'already,' su la groppa = 'onto the back' and del fiero animale = 'of the fierce animal.'
segments.inferno-17-g76: e disse a me is straightforward narrative: e = 'and'; disse is the past (passato remoto/simple past) of dire 'said/told'; a me = 'to me' (indirect object). It introduces the quoted speech that follows.
segments.inferno-17-g77: The quoted command 'Or sie forte e ardito' contains archaic/poetic forms: Or = 'now'; sie is an older/poetic subjunctive/optative form of essere (modern sia) used here as a jussive ('be'); forte e ardito are adjectives 'strong and bold.' So this is an exhortation: 'Now be strong and daring.'
segments.inferno-17-g78: Omai = 'by now.' Si scende is an impersonal or reflexive construction meaning 'one descends' / 'people descend.' Per introduces the means ('by/through') and sì fatte is a demonstrative + past participle combination ('such made,' i.e. 'such constructed') modifying scale 'stairs.'
segments.inferno-17-g79: Monta here is an imperative or hortative form 'climb!' / 'go up!' addressed to someone; dinanzi is an adverb 'in front / ahead' specifying where to climb.
segments.inferno-17-g80: Ch’ is the clitic form of che used causally or explanatorily ('for / because'); i' = io 'I' (here contracted i'); voglio esser is the present 'I want to be'; mezzo is a predicate noun 'middle' meaning 'in the middle (of the line).' The whole clause gives the speaker's motive for the order.
segments.inferno-17-g81: Sì che introduces a result/purpose clause and is followed by the subjunctive possa ('may / be able to'), expressing the intended/resulting prevention. La coda is the subject 'the tail,' non + possa + far male = 'so that it cannot do harm' (far male = idiom 'to do harm').
segments.inferno-17-g82: Qual è colui che is a simile formula ('as is the one who.../like he who...') with colui 'that one' and a relative clause introduced by che. Sì presso ha 'l riprezzo uses sì + presso + avere ('has the shudder near') to mean 'is close to the shudder/chill' (riprezzo = a shudder/qualm), and de la quartana specifies the kind (the quartan fever).
segments.inferno-17-g83: C' ha is a contracted form (here equivalent to 'che ha' or 'who has'); già is the adverb 'already'; l’unghie ('the nails') is a plural noun with the article, and smorte is an adjective 'pale / lifeless' describing them. The clause means 'who already has pale nails.'
segments.inferno-17-g84: E triema tutto pur guardando ’l rezzo: triema is an archaic poetic form meaning 'trembles' (tremare); tutto pur = 'altogether / still' intensifies the trembling; guardando is a gerund 'while looking at,' and ’l rezzo = 'the shade/coolness' (rezzo = cool shade). The whole line depicts tremor at the sight of cooling shade.
segments.inferno-17-g85: Tal = 'such' (so/that kind of); divenn’ is an abbreviated past tense of divenire/diventare ('I became' or 'so I became'), with io 'I' as subject. A le parole porte is a poetic locution 'at the doors/threshold of words' (meaning 'on the verge of speaking' or 'at the point of the words').
segments.inferno-17-g86: Ma contrasts what follows. Vergogna mi fé is a causative construction: fé (past of fare) 'made/caused' with mi as the indirect object ('shame made me [do/feel something]'). Le sue minacce ('his threats') are the cause/content producing that shame. The syntax places the emotion (vergogna) frontally for emphasis.
segments.inferno-17-g87: Che introduces a clause explaining the result: innanzi a buon segnor = 'before a good lord' (innanzi = in front of/before). Fa servo forte is an idiomatic verblike phrase meaning 'makes a servant strong' — i.e. a servant puts on a bold face before his master; fa is present tense 'makes/causes.'

sentences.inferno-17-s1: “Behold the beast with the pointed tail, which crosses mountains and breaks through walls and weapons!
sentences.inferno-17-s2: Behold her who makes the whole world stink!”
sentences.inferno-17-s4: Thus my guide began to speak to me; and he signaled to it to come to the bank, near the end of the marble stones we had walked upon.
sentences.inferno-17-s9: Its whole tail was writhing in the void, twisting upward the venomous fork that armed its point like a scorpion’s.
sentences.inferno-17-s10: The guide said, “Now our path must turn aside a little as far as that wicked beast that lies over yonder.”
sentences.inferno-17-s11: Therefore we went down to the right breast, and we took ten steps along the outer edge, so as to keep well clear of the sand and the little flame.
sentences.inferno-17-s12: And when we had come to it, a little farther on I saw people sitting on the sand, near the diminished place.
sentences.inferno-17-s5: And that filthy image of fraud came on, and brought its head and torso to the shore, but did not draw its tail up onto the bank.
sentences.inferno-17-s6: Its face was the face of a just man, so kindly did it have its outward skin; and all the rest of its trunk was that of a serpent. It had two hairy claws as far as the armpits; its back and breast and both its sides it had painted with knots and little roundels.
sentences.inferno-17-s7: With more colors, underlaid and overlaid, neither Tartars nor Turks ever made cloths, nor were such webs ever set on the loom by Arachne.
sentences.inferno-17-s8: As barges sometimes lie at the shore, partly in the water and partly on land, and as there among the gluttonous Germans the beaver settles itself to wage its war, so the most wicked beast was stationed on the rim that is of stone and confines the sand.
sentences.inferno-17-s21: Now go away; and since you are still alive, know that my neighbor Vitaliano will sit here at my left side.
sentences.inferno-17-s22: Among these Florentines I am a Paduan: many times my ears deafen me, shouting, "May the sovereign knight come, who will bring the purse with three beaks!"
sentences.inferno-17-s24: Here he contorted his mouth and thrust out his tongue, like an ox that licks its nose.
sentences.inferno-17-s13: There my master said to me, "So that you may carry away the full experience of this circuit, go and see their company."
sentences.inferno-17-s14: Let your remarks be brief there; while you return, I will speak with this one, that she may grant us her strong shoulders.
sentences.inferno-17-s15: Thus again I went up alone along the extreme crest of that seventh circle, where the sorrowful people sat.
sentences.inferno-17-s16: Their grief burst forth from their eyes; here and there they reached out with their hands — sometimes toward the vapours, and sometimes toward the hot ground: dogs in summer do no differently, now with the muzzle, now with the paw, when they are bitten by fleas or flies or horseflies.
sentences.inferno-17-s33: Which was mine, when I saw that I was in the air on every side, and I saw every sight extinguished save that of the beast.
sentences.inferno-17-s34: She moves off, going slowly, slowly; she wheels and descends, but I do not notice it except that she blows on my face and beneath me.
sentences.inferno-17-s35: I already felt from the right hand the whirlpool making beneath us a terrible crash, wherefore with my eyes downward I thrust my head forward.
sentences.inferno-17-s36: Then I was more timid at the brink, because I saw fires and heard lamentations; wherefore I, trembling, crouched up completely.
sentences.inferno-17-s37: And then I saw, for I had not seen it ahead, the descending and the circling through the great evils that were drawing near from different quarters.
sentences.inferno-17-s38: As the falcon that has long been on its wings, which, without seeing lure or quarry, makes the falconer say, 'Alas,'
sentences.inferno-17-s39: , it descends downwards whence it moves nimble, by a hundred wheels, and it places itself far from its master; disdainful and fell; thus Geryon set us at the bottom at the very foot of the sheer rock, and, having unloaded our people, he vanished like from a cord's notch.
sentences.inferno-17-s17: Afterward, when certain eyes were turned toward his face — in which the sorrowful flame falls — I recognized none of them; but I noticed that from each neck there hung a little pouch which had a definite color and mark, and so it seems their eye feasts on it.
sentences.inferno-17-s18: And as I, looking among them, came along, in a yellow bag I saw an azure one that had the face and bearing of a lion.
sentences.inferno-17-s19: Then, as the course of my gaze continued, I saw another, as red as blood, showing a goose whiter than butter.
sentences.inferno-17-s20: And one who had marked his white little pouch with a blue and fat sow said to me: "What are you doing in this pit?"
sentences.inferno-17-s29: I seated myself upon those broad shoulders; I meant to say so, but the voice did not come as I had thought: "Make that you embrace me."
sentences.inferno-17-s30: But he — who at another time perhaps had come to my mind for something else — as soon as I had mounted, clasped me with his arms and supported me; and he said, "Geryon, move now: the broad wheels, and let the descent be slight; think of the new load you have."
sentences.inferno-17-s31: Just as a little ship backs out of its berth, moving back and back, so it withdrew from there; and when it felt itself completely at ease, it turned its tail toward where the chest was, and that stretched part moved like an eel, and with its claws it drew the air to itself.
sentences.inferno-17-s32: I do not think there was any greater fear than when Phaethon let go of the reins, whereby the sky, as it still seems, was seamed; nor when wretched Icarus felt his loins plucked by the heated wax, his father crying to him, "You take a bad way!"
sentences.inferno-17-s25: And I, fearing lest by staying any longer I should vex him — he who a little while before had admonished me — turned back to the weary souls.
sentences.inferno-17-s26: My guide, who had already climbed up onto the back of the fierce animal, found me and said to me: 'Now be strong and daring.'
sentences.inferno-17-s27: By now one descends such stairs; climb ahead — I want to be in the middle — so that the tail cannot do any harm.
sentences.inferno-17-s28: As is the man who is so near the chill of the quartan fever, who already has his nails pale, and trembles entirely even while looking at the coolness — such was I at the threshold of words; but shame made me obey his threats, for before a good lord a servant acts bravely.
